parts.prompt: Write the half-reaction for the oxidation that occurs at the anode.
parts.scoring_notes: Al is oxidized at the anode in the Al/Zn galvanic cell. State symbols not required.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced oxidation half-reaction at the anode: 1 point
wrong_examples: Writing a reduction half-reaction at the anode — oxidation occurs at the anode.
common_traps: Anode = oxidation (loss of electrons); cathode = reduction (gain of electrons). Al is the stronger reducing agent (more negative E°) so it is oxidized.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 01: For the correct equation (state symbols not required): \ce{Al(s) -> Al^3+(aq) + 3e-}.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly writes the oxidation half-reaction with Al losing 3 electrons to form Al³⁺. The anode is where oxidation occurs. Earns the point.
This is the reduction half-reaction (Al³⁺ gaining electrons to form Al metal), not oxidation. At the anode, the metal is oxidized (loses electrons). No credit.

parts.prompt: Write the balanced net ionic equation for the overall reaction that occurs in the galvanic cell.
parts.scoring_notes: Must balance electrons: Al loses 3e⁻ per atom (×2 = 6e⁻); Zn²⁺ gains 2e⁻ per ion (×3 = 6e⁻). State symbols not required.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced net ionic equation with electrons canceled: 1 point
wrong_examples: Writing 1 Al + 1 Zn²⁺ → 1 Al³⁺ + 1 Zn — electrons are not balanced.
Including spectator ions (e.g., NO₃⁻) — net ionic equation excludes spectators.
common_traps: Electron balance requires LCM(3,2) = 6 electrons: multiply Al half-reaction by 2 and Zn half-reaction by 3.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 02: For the correct balanced net ionic equation (state symbols not required): \ce{2 Al(s) + 3 Zn^2+(aq) -> 2 Al^3+(aq) + 3 Zn(s)}.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly balances electrons by multiplying the Al half-reaction by 2 (6e⁻) and the Zn half-reaction by 3 (6e⁻), then cancels electrons to give the net ionic equation. Earns the point.
Electrons are not balanced: Al loses 3e⁻ but Zn²⁺ gains only 2e⁻. The equation must be multiplied to achieve electron balance (LCM = 6). No credit.

parts.prompt:
Initially, each electrode has a mass of 50.0 g. The cell is allowed to run for a period of time
and is then stopped. Which electrode's mass changed the most? Justify your answer with a
calculation.

Reduction Half-Reaction Ec V

Au3+(aq)+ 3 e - " Au(s) +1.50
Zn2+(aq)+ 2 e - " Zn(s) -0.76
Mn2+(aq)+ 2 e - " Mn(s) -1.19
Al3+(aq)+ 3 e - " Al(s) -1.66
Be2+(aq)+ 2 e - " Be(s) -1.85
parts.scoring_notes: For every 6 mol e⁻ transferred: 2 mol Al dissolved (mass change per 6e⁻: 2×27.0 = 54.0 g) vs. 3 mol Zn deposited (mass change per 6e⁻: 3×65.4 = 196.2 g). Zn electrode gains more mass than Al loses. Accept any correct comparison.

scoring_points.description: For correctly identifying the Zn electrode with a valid comparative calculation: 1 point

required_elements: Per 6 mol electrons: Al electrode loses $2 \times 27.0 = 54.0$ g; Zn electrode gains $3 \times 65.4 = 196.2$ g.
The Zn electrode undergoes the greater mass change.
alternatives.correct_example:
For every 6 mol of electrons transferred: 
Al anode loses: $2\text{ mol Al} \times 27.0\text{ g/mol} = 54.0\text{ g}$ 
Zn cathode gains: $3\text{ mol Zn} \times 65.4\text{ g/mol} = 196.2\text{ g}$ 
The Zn electrode's mass changes the most (gains 196.2 g per 6 mol e⁻ vs. Al losing 54.0 g).

wrong_examples: Saying the Al electrode changes more — Zn has a much higher molar mass and more moles deposit per mole of electrons.
common_traps: Must compare molar mass × stoichiometric coefficient for each electrode per unit charge transferred.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 03: For the correct answer and a valid justification that correctly compares the masses of Al and Zn based on their molar masses and the stoichiometry of the balanced equation. Zn experiences a greater change in mass. For every 6 mol electrons transferred: Zn cathode gains $3\text{ mol} \times 65.38\text{ g/mol} = 196\text{ g}$; Al anode loses $2\text{ mol} \times 26.98\text{ g/mol} = 54.0\text{ g}$.

sample_responses.response:
The Zn electrode experiences the greater mass change. For every 6 mol of electrons transferred: 
Al anode loses: $2\text{ mol} \times 27.0\text{ g/mol} = 54.0\text{ g}$ 
Zn cathode gains: $3\text{ mol} \times 65.4\text{ g/mol} = 196\text{ g}$ 
Since 196 g > 54 g, the Zn electrode changes more.
sample_responses.commentary: Per 6 mol electrons transferred, correctly calculates and compares the mass gain at the Zn cathode (196 g) versus mass loss at the Al anode (54 g). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The Al electrode changes more because aluminum has a smaller molar mass, so more moles dissolve.
sample_responses.commentary: A smaller molar mass means fewer grams per mole, not more grams changing. For every 6 electrons, only 2 mol Al dissolves (54 g), while 3 mol Zn deposits (196 g). The Zn electrode changes more. No credit.

parts.prompt:
The standard
Zn/Al
cell has a value of E c
%
ell equal to 0.90 V. The scientist needs a galvanic cell
that produces a greater voltage. The scientist has access to the chemical systems in the table.
Zn
If the scientist uses the half-cell and one of the other options from the table, what is the
MAXIMUM voltage that could be generated at standard conditions?

C $H_2O$ -
parts.scoring_notes: Maximizing voltage: pair Zn (oxidized, E°_ox = +0.76 V) with the half-cell with the highest reduction potential from the table. Au³⁺/Au has E° = +1.50 V. E°_cell = E°_cathode − E°_anode = 1.50 − (−0.76) = 2.26 V.

scoring_points.description: For the correct maximum voltage (2.26 V) using Zn/Au pair: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
To maximize voltage, pair Zn (anode) with the half-cell with the highest reduction potential: Au³⁺/Au ($E° = +1.50\text{ V}$). 
$E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}} = 1.50\text{ V} - (-0.76\text{ V}) = 2.26\text{ V}$

wrong_examples: Using Be²⁺/Be as cathode — it has a more negative E°, which would give a lower cell voltage.
Adding the reduction potentials directly without reversing the anode.
common_traps: $E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode}}$ (both as reduction potentials); equivalently, $E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{red,cathode}} + E°_{\text{ox,anode}}$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 04: For the correct calculated value. Accept: $E°_{\text{cell}} = 1.50\text{ V} + 0.76\text{ V} = 2.26\text{ V}$. OR: Au³⁺(aq) + 3e⁻ → Au(s) +1.50 V; Zn(s) → Zn²⁺(aq) + 2e⁻ +0.76 V; $E°_{\text{cell}} = 2.26\text{ V}$.

sample_responses.response:
To maximize voltage, pair Zn (anode, $E°_{\text{ox}} = +0.76\text{ V}$) with Au³⁺/Au (cathode, $E°_{\text{red}} = +1.50\text{ V}$): 
$E°_{\text{cell}} = E°_{\text{cathode}} - E°_{\text{anode,red}} = 1.50 - (-0.76) = 2.26\text{ V}$
sample_responses.commentary: Selects Au³⁺/Au (highest reduction potential in the table) as the cathode and correctly calculates the cell potential. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: To maximize voltage, pair Zn with Be²⁺/Be: $E°_{\text{cell}} = -1.85 - (-0.76) = -1.09\text{ V}$
sample_responses.commentary: Be²⁺/Be has a very negative reduction potential. Using it as the cathode while Zn is the anode would require more negative cathode E° than anode — this would actually give a spontaneous cell in the reverse direction (negative cell potential as written). The maximum voltage comes from the cathode with the highest reduction potential (Au³⁺/Au). No credit.
